course_description:
This course deals in depth with the process of international commercial arbitration, which is one of the major growth areas of legal practice and the most widely used dispute resolution method in international business.

This course considers arbitration as a means of dispute resolution for international commercial disputes. The course will provide a practical perspective of the lifecycle of an arbitration, from the making and drafting of an arbitration agreement to the enforcement of an award in a national court. The course will be split into three parts. The course will first analyse the basic concepts of international commercial arbitration and key instruments and arbitral institutions. It will then consider the arbitration agreement in depth, including the considerations when drafting an arbitration agreement, and how to enforce an arbitration agreement in the face of legal proceedings. The course then addresses the practice and procedure of an arbitration, including the process of the arbitration itself and the steps after an award is given by the tribunal.

restrictions: LLM, LLM(Adv), LLM(16), LLM(24), MICLaw(16) Quota: Minimum of 12 enrolments

course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims

The aim of the course is to provide an understanding of the law and practice of international commercial arbitration.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the basic concepts in international commercial arbitration.
Understand the laws and legal instruments that may be applicable to an international commercial arbitration.
Draft an appropriate form of international arbitration agreement/clause.
Understand the relationship between litigation and an agreement to arbitrate, and the legal issues raised when enforcing an agreement to arbitrate.
Understand the implications of the selection of an arbitral institution, seat/venue, and choice of laws issues.
Understand generally how to commence and conduct an international commercial arbitration;
Understand generally how to conduct a hearing in an international arbitration;
Understand the legal rights that the parties (for enforcement and challenge) in relation to an arbitral award.

assessment_details.title: Case Note
assessment_details.task_description: Students will be required to prepare a case note that describes and analyses a significant decision dealing with international commercial arbitration. Students will be provided with a list of cases to choose from on the first day of the course. The word limit for the essay is a maximum of 2,500 words any material beyond that will not be read, and penalties will be applied of 1 mark per 100 words over the word limit. By way of clarification, this 2,500 words: includes headings; does not include text in the header or word count; and does not include footnotes - however footnotes should be used for citation purposes only. Any substantive text that appears in the footnote will not be read and will not contribute to your mark. For style and referencing, students should refer to the Australian Guide to Legal Citation . See item 3.4 of this course profile for further details. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The assignment must be submitted electronically via the assessment submission link on the course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) site.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
The maximum extension for an assessment item due within the semester / teaching period in which the course is offered is 7 days. In exceptional circumstances, an extension may be approved for a further 7 days. Where a student is incapacitated for a period longer than 14 days, they are advised to apply for removal of course.
A student may apply for an extension to assessment due date if they are unable to meet an assessment deadline due to extenuating circumstances. Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply. Additional information on extensions can be found within the Assessment Procedure .
Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.
A request for an extension to an assessment due date must be submitted through my.UQ as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time . Requests for extensions received after the application due date and time, must include evidence of the reason for the late request, and will require the decision maker listed in the Decision-Maker Tables in the Student Grievance Resolution Procedure to determine whether to accept the request for consideration.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Penalties for Late Submission
The following penalties apply to late submission of an assessment item, assuming that an extension has not been approved, and that late submission is academically possible (e.g., it does not delay feedback to a cohort, or interfere with course delivery).
First 7 periods of 24 hours (or part thereof) - 10% per 24 hours of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item. For example, a report worth a maximum of 40 marks, submitted 28 hours late will attract a penalty of 8 marks, calculated as 2 periods of 24 hours x 10% x 40 marks.
More than 7 periods of 24 hours - 100%.

assessment_details.title: End of Semester Exam
assessment_details.task_description: Individual work The Exam must be the individual student’s own work and the following conditions apply: Students are not to consult with any other person in relation to the questions on the assessment. Students may consult with the Course Coordinator if clarification of any question is thought necessary. The Course Coordinator may circulate clarifications to the entire class if necessary or appropriate. Students are required to sign a declaration that the answers submitted are their own, and that they have not consulted with any other person in relation to the assessment. Answers submitted without this declaration will not be accepted. Where it is established that a student has consulted with any other person in relation to the questions on the assessment, that student shall be awarded a mark of ‘0’ even if the student’s answers would otherwise meet the criteria for any higher mark. Format and Length The assessment answers should include all of the following on the title page: The student’s name and number; A declaration that the answers are the student’s own work and that he or she has not consulted with any other person (This is very important); and The word count. The Exam will be a mixture of short answer questions, essay questions and problem questions. Details of the word limit will be provided on the assessment paper; penalties will be applied for assessment papers that go over the word limit, with information provided on the assessment paper. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Day 1 Introductions; What is arbitration? How is it different from other ADR processes? Advantages and disadvantages of arbitration; legal taxonomy and nature of arbitration. Key institutions and the development of the system of international commercial arbitration. Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Day 2 Essential legal concepts - the arbitration agreement, arbitrability, separability, competence, seat and venue
Day 3 Drafting an arbitration agreement - importance of good drafting; essential components; other matters to consider. Learning outcomes: L03

Day 4 Enforcing the arbitration agreement - Australian law; applications for a stay. Practice and procedure. The commencement of the arbitral process - notices of dispute; establishing the arbitral tribunal; independence and impartiality.
Day 5 Jurisdiction of an arbitral tribunal; challenges to jurisdiction Interim measures - powers of the tribunal; powers of national courts; enforcement of interim measures
Day 6 The final award - form and content; effect of an award Challenging an award - limited rights of challenge Learning outcomes: L08
Day 7 Enforcement of award - issues arising on enforcement; grounds for refusal to enforce Learning outcomes: L08
Day 8 Confidentiality in arbitration; global survey of jurisdictions Different types of arbitration - investor-State arbitrations
